title: The Old Stockman's Lament
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Wrap me up in me stockwhip and blanket,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And bury me deep down below,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Where this piffle and sham wonן¿½t disgust me,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In the land where the coolibahs grow;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For Iן¿½ve stayed with some well-to-do people,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Iן¿½ve dined with some middle-class folk;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Iן¿½ve sorrowed by clock-tower and steeple
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Till my heart for the Commonwealthן¿½s broke.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They have flown in another direction,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Who used to clack-clack by the hour
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of ן¿½this awful Freetrade and Protection,ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of our dear darling member ן¿½in power,ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the Higher Religion for Dossers,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the Need of an Object for Drunks,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Now theyן¿½re all of them Red or Blue Crossers,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With their tails sticking out of their trunks.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ There are citified Martins in dozens,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Darling Point Martins the pick,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Who used to be horrified cousins
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of a Martin we knew as ן¿½Mad Mick.ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He is hanging out somewhere where French is;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But they heard heן¿½d enlisted, somehow,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And ן¿½twould paralyse Mick in the trenches
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To know how heן¿½s glorified now.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You remember the George Henry Crosses?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Theyן¿½ve packed up twelve trunks in despair.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Heן¿½s the boss of the back-station bosses,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Ernieן¿½s the son and the heir.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He has never put hands on a wether,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Nor heard a pithed store-bullock grunt;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So theyן¿½re taking the mailboat to England
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To see Ernie safe to the Front.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And each of the war-going parsons
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Costs many a heart-breaking tear,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Like that caddish young cub of old Carsonן¿½s,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ All found and four hundred a year.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He feels not a word that he preaches,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But he will not be criticised there,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Where, out where the flying shell screeches,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Poor Tommy must fight, sweat and swear.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Our relatives, tooן¿½ (hang the Censor!)
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Each girl has a tear on her cheek.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Cousin Roger has gone as dispenser
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (Expenses and three pounds a week.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ More risky than listן¿½ning to sermons,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As some of our fellows will find,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Is a fierce fortnightן¿½s fight with the Germans
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In front, and with Roger behind.)

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the Girls, they are writing like blazes,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Auntie is moaning like hell;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And I wish I was under the daisies,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Or the bluegum would do just as well.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So I want to be wropped in me blanket,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And buried down, deep down, below;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Where this cant and this cackle wonן¿½t reach me,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In the land where the coolibahs grow.
meaning: About: Henry Lawson (17 June 1867 - 2 September 1922) was an Australian writer and poet. Along with his contemporary Banjo Paterson, Lawson is among the best-known Australian poets and fiction writers of the colonial period.
